0: <title>Environmental Assessment of Two Irrigation Systems in an Organic Tomato Crop System Under Manure Compost Fertilization: a Sustainable Circular Economy Approach in Catalonia (Spain) </title>
Roberto Quirós ¹,2 Anthony Halog 3 Pere Munoz²
Received: 4 August 2021 / Accepted: 12 February 2022 © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2022
1: The circular economy is proposed as a promising strategy for both dealing with the cur- rent environmental issues and providing socio-economic benefits. The transformation of organic waste materials into a reusable product for crops is a way to contribute to the change from a linear economy to a circular model. Manure reuse as fertilizer is the most adequate option for the management of such material. This study aims to highlight the environmental impact assessment of two irrigation systems (i.e. integrated and dripping) of a tomato crop fertilized with manure compost and the integration of life assessment methodology with a circular economy. The life cycle assessment methodology was used to calculate the environmental impacts through the whole life cycle. Life cycle assessment is a methodology to assess the environmental performance of a product system in a circu- lar approach. The research focused on the climate change impact category and the water applied to crops to know the effect on the yield of fruits. Overall, comparing two crop seasons, it was observed that a greater water supply contributed to higher yield fruit for the two irrigation systems studied. On the other hand, in regard to the environmental impacts, it was observed that the integrated system showed a better environmental performance than the dripping system for all categories assessed. Considering that livestock manure is trans- formed into organic fertilizer which is reintroduced into the agronomical system through the application to a tomato crop, a circularity indicator of 70% (organic fertilizer from the composting process X total mass of manure-1 was obtained in this agronomic system.
Roberto Quirós Roberto.quiros_v@ucr.ac.cr Anthony Halog a.halog@uq.edu.au Pere Munoz Pere.munoz@uab.cat

1 School of Industrial Engineering, University of Costa Rica, San José, Costa Rica 2060 2 Sostenipra Research Group, Institut of Environmental Science and Technology (ICTA), University Autonomous of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain 3 Research Group in Industrial Ecology and Circular Economy, School of Earth and Environmental Science, Faculty of Science, University of Queensland, Brisbane, QLD 4072, Australia
Keywords Manure . Life cycle assessment . Irrigation . Tomato . Compost . Water

1 School of Industrial Engineering, University of Costa Rica, San José, Costa Rica 2060 2 Sostenipra Research Group, Institut of Environmental Science and Technology (ICTA), University Autonomous of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain 3 Research Group in Industrial Ecology and Circular Economy, School of Earth and Environmental Science, Faculty of Science, University of Queensland, Brisbane, QLD 4072, Australia
Keywords Manure . Life cycle assessment . Irrigation . Tomato . Compost . Water
3: According to the circular economy (CE) action plan adopted by the European Commis- sion (EC) in 2015, in a CE, the value of products, materials, and resources is maintained in the economy for as long as possible, and the generation of waste minimized to develop a sustainable, low carbon, resource-efficient, and competitive economy. It makes it necessary to completely rethink the way of producing and consuming, and to transform waste into value-added products, with the final aims of slowing, narrowing, and closing the resource loop [1].

Chemical fertilizers increased the yield of crops but also, they are great pollutants con- tributors to the environment. Intensive inorganic fertilizer usage in agriculture caused SO many health problems and unrecoverable environmental pollution. To reduce and eliminate the adverse effects of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides on human health and the envi- ronment, new agricultural practices have been developed in the so-called organic agri- culture, ecological agriculture, or sustainable agriculture [2, 3]. Since the early 2000s, several efforts have concentrated on the transition from the traditional linear production ("take-make-use-dispose of") to a CE, proposed as a promising strategy for both dealing with the current environmental issues and providing socio-economic benefits [4].

The use of organic fertilizer in crops or soil amend for its restoration contributes to the current sustainability thinking promoted by the CE discipline. Compost can play a key role in the bioeconomy towards the best environmental agro-wastes into profitable resources. An excellent example is done by the diffusion worldwide of bio-degradable plastics made of starch-based polymeric composite material [5]. The main sources of organic fertilizers are composted livestock manures, plant residues, and industrial wastes. The organic fertilizers provide the nutritional requirements of plants and also suppress the plant pest populations [6]. As mentioned in several studies, organic fer- tilizers increase the yield and quality of crops in ways like inorganic fertilizers [7, 8]. The use of organic fertilizers is a major component of organic farming practices [9]. Manures, a kind of organic fertilizer, can provide the essential plant nutrients and enhance crop productivity, but also leave a beneficial residual effect on succeeding crops [10]. The growing public concerns for human health due to the undesirable effects of hazardous synthetic chemicals and the massive use of mineral fertilizers have encour- aged farmers to search for safer and eco-friendly alternatives [11]. Besides the possibil- ity to reduce or even avoid potential risks for human health due to environmental and hazardous contaminants [12]. Pergola et al. [13] have instead questioned that another fundamental criterion should be routinely adopted to sustain the on-farm green compost production chains from agricultural waste. Various kinds of organic materials such as animal manures, sewage sludge, and crop residues are applied to soil to improve soil organic matter content and consequently, the physical, chemical, and biological prop- erties of the soil [14]. Livestock manure generation in Catalonia is about 19 million tons per year, which is equivalent to more than 100 million kilograms of nitrogen per year [15]. The livestock sector contributes to the livelihood of millions of people, but their production poses several environmental challenges such as greenhouse gas emis- sions, eutrophication, acidification, and biodiversity loss [16]. Therefore, to reduce the

environmental impact, some farmers are taking advantage of the manure. Manure is a livestock residue that has little commercial value and is responsible for the environ- mental impacts of several categories [17]. Introducing manure as a resource to increase the value of the waste means one of the main principles of CE. Manure also has value to improvements in soil quality and productivity, for example, in building up organic matter or improving soil water retention, which often is difficult to quantify and are variable based upon past soil management practice [18]. Additionally, the use of manure to produce a kind of organic fertilizer increases the value for society of a residue which normally lixiviates to soil and groundwater. In most cases, manure is used as a valu- able resource because of its nutrient value, particularly as a source of the major nutri- ents of N and P, none of the current approaches can properly reflect this benefit that drives farmers to accept manure to be applied in crop as mineral fertilizers substitute. In the present study, organic fertilizer from manure was applied to a tomato cropping system in Spain to assess the environmental performance of two fertirrigation systems (integrated and dripping), and the yield obtained in both agricultural practices was also considered in the study. The use of manure compost in tomato production (Solanum Lycopersicum L.) may result in changes not only in the production yield but also in fruit growth and fruit quality. At the same time, the tomato phenolic profile may be affected [19]. Many aspects of sustainability and circularity are essential when a study is made, for example, in our case study, the quality of fruits to improve and preserve the health of human beings (social), a cost reduction for farmers (economical), and a reduced environmental impact (environment). Tomato phenolics deserve special atten- tion for human health given that these compounds with antioxidant properties have been implicated in metabolic processes that may be important in reducing cancer and cardio- vascular disease risk [20, 21]. Tomatoes are the major dietary source of the antioxidant lycopene, which has been linked to many health benefits, including reduced risk of heart disease and cancer. The tomato considered in this study was Lycopersicon esculentum Var. Punxa is a traditional variety consumed in the region of Catalunya. The objective of this research is the environmental assessment of two irrigation systems (i.e. integrated and dripping) for a tomato crop fertilized with a kind of compost from cattle-manure considering relevant aspects related to LCA with the CE approach. Also, a statistical approach was developed to know the significance of two key variables (water applied and yield) related to the crops. For the environmental assessment, the life cycle assess- ment methodology (LCA) and ISO 14044 guidelines were used to calculate the impacts related to the two irrigation systems. The same methodology was used to assess the environmental performance and crop yield in open-field tomato crops [22]. LCA is a widely used tool to assess the environmental impacts of livestock supply chains and resultant products [23-25]. Theoretical foundations have arisen in science to link LCA and CE to understand sustainability in agricultural systems. Scheepens et al. [26] pro- posed an LCA-based metric for products as an indicator for circularity; however, the proposed method deals with the reduction of externalities and not with measuring the degree of circularity. Similarly [27] used a lifecycle perspective for comparing supply chain performance in chemical and food sectors, integrating the CE within sustainable principles. Di Maio and Rem [28] developed the Circular Economy Index (CEI), includ- ing also environmental and economic aspects. A literature review demonstrated a lack of studies of a cropping system (i.e. tomato crop) fertilized with compost-manure to know the environmental impacts and its contribution during the whole life cycle. Also,

a lack of studies in literature was observed to determine a circularity indicator for this kind of agricultural system. Therefore, in this study, a circular indicator (output mass/ input mass) was calculated to know how circular is the system.

[13] have instead questioned that another fundamental criterion should be routinely adopted to sustain the on-farm green compost production chains from agricultural waste. Various kinds of organic materials such as animal manures, sewage sludge, and crop residues are applied to soil to improve soil organic matter content and consequently, the physical, chemical, and biological prop- erties of the soil [14]. Livestock manure generation in Catalonia is about 19 million tons per year, which is equivalent to more than 100 million kilograms of nitrogen per year [15]. The livestock sector contributes to the livelihood of millions of people, but their production poses several environmental challenges such as greenhouse gas emis- sions, eutrophication, acidification, and biodiversity loss [16]. Therefore, to reduce the

environmental impact, some farmers are taking advantage of the manure. Manure is a livestock residue that has little commercial value and is responsible for the environ- mental impacts of several categories [17]. Introducing manure as a resource to increase the value of the waste means one of the main principles of CE. Manure also has value to improvements in soil quality and productivity, for example, in building up organic matter or improving soil water retention, which often is difficult to quantify and are variable based upon past soil management practice [18]. Additionally, the use of manure to produce a kind of organic fertilizer increases the value for society of a residue which normally lixiviates to soil and groundwater. In most cases, manure is used as a valu- able resource because of its nutrient value, particularly as a source of the major nutri- ents of N and P, none of the current approaches can properly reflect this benefit that drives farmers to accept manure to be applied in crop as mineral fertilizers substitute. In the present study, organic fertilizer from manure was applied to a tomato cropping system in Spain to assess the environmental performance of two fertirrigation systems (integrated and dripping), and the yield obtained in both agricultural practices was also considered in the study. The use of manure compost in tomato production (Solanum Lycopersicum L.) may result in changes not only in the production yield but also in fruit growth and fruit quality. At the same time, the tomato phenolic profile may be affected [19]. Many aspects of sustainability and circularity are essential when a study is made, for example, in our case study, the quality of fruits to improve and preserve the health of human beings (social), a cost reduction for farmers (economical), and a reduced environmental impact (environment). Tomato phenolics deserve special atten- tion for human health given that these compounds with antioxidant properties have been implicated in metabolic processes that may be important in reducing cancer and cardio- vascular disease risk [20, 21]. Tomatoes are the major dietary source of the antioxidant lycopene, which has been linked to many health benefits, including reduced risk of heart disease and cancer. The tomato considered in this study was Lycopersicon esculentum Var. Punxa is a traditional variety consumed in the region of Catalunya. The objective of this research is the environmental assessment of two irrigation systems (i.e. integrated and dripping) for a tomato crop fertilized with a kind of compost from cattle-manure considering relevant aspects related to LCA with the CE approach. Also, a statistical approach was developed to know the significance of two key variables (water applied and yield) related to the crops. For the environmental assessment, the life cycle assess- ment methodology (LCA) and ISO 14044 guidelines were used to calculate the impacts related to the two irrigation systems. The same methodology was used to assess the environmental performance and crop yield in open-field tomato crops [22]. LCA is a widely used tool to assess the environmental impacts of livestock supply chains and resultant products [23-25]. Theoretical foundations have arisen in science to link LCA and CE to understand sustainability in agricultural systems. Scheepens et al. [26] pro- posed an LCA-based metric for products as an indicator for circularity; however, the proposed method deals with the reduction of externalities and not with measuring the degree of circularity. Similarly [27] used a lifecycle perspective for comparing supply chain performance in chemical and food sectors, integrating the CE within sustainable principles. Di Maio and Rem [28] developed the Circular Economy Index (CEI), includ- ing also environmental and economic aspects. A literature review demonstrated a lack of studies of a cropping system (i.e. tomato crop) fertilized with compost-manure to know the environmental impacts and its contribution during the whole life cycle. Also,

a lack of studies in literature was observed to determine a circularity indicator for this kind of agricultural system. Therefore, in this study, a circular indicator (output mass/ input mass) was calculated to know how circular is the system.
8: This section contains a broad explanation of the LCA which is the methodology used to calculate the environmental impacts of the agronomical system. All the steps of the LCA are broadly explained and adapted for the experimental study. The methodology used to carry out the study is according to several LCA studies [22, 29, 30]. Also, the circular- ity indicator is calculated to know how circular is the process. To evaluate the sustain- ability of the environmental impact of GHG emissions, energy consumption for mixing and aeration, and production cost of the green compost production chains, several LCA models were applied in many papers reviewed by [13]. These models take into considera- tion the manure processing technologies along the entire life cycle of manure and related end products, industrial composting and waste management systems, biowaste treatments, and food-waste home composting systems. As shown in Fig. 1, the most famous and wide- spread circular model archetype is the Ellen MacArthur Foundation [1] diagram. This model is characterized by the division between biological and technical materials, which, with their cycles, provide the scheme with a particular shape, also called "CE butterfly dia- gram." In particular, the CE concept is based on natural principles: it takes insights from living systems, is characterized by adaptability and resilience, and follows the cascading of materials.
LCA to calculate the impacts over the life cycle of a waste (i.e., manure) and the bio- logical transformation of manure (i.e. organic fertilizer) to reintroduce into the productive
Fig. 1 CE diagram that illustrates the biological and technical material cycle (in the upper part) and the underlying circular patterns that can create more value from resources [1]

system (i.e. tomato crop). As we know, the manure is a kind of waste that generally ends in landfills or verted to rivers. Both, LCA and CE are useful strategies and tools to analyze sustainability and circularity in agricultural systems.

LCA methodology was performed to calculate the environmental impacts of a tomato crop fertilized with organic fertilizer from livestock manure considering the entire life cycle (production, transportation, and application to crops). Also, some statistics methods were used to know the significance of two critical variables related to crops (i.e. water applied and yield.). LCA is a scientific and robust methodology to know the environmental impacts caused by a product system during the whole life cycle from the material extraction to final disposition and its reincorporation to the natural ecosystem. The ISO 14044 was used as the mainframe to do the environmental assessment of the crops. The ISO 14044 states four main steps to design an LCA: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact calcula- tion, and interpretation. The present experiment was led through the four steps mentioned.

The goal and scope definition are key points in an LCA. The goal is related to the intended application, the reason for carrying out the study, and the intended audience, among others [31]. The scope is related to relevant aspects of the LCA (function, func- tional unit, system boundaries, methodological aspects, etc.). In our case study, the goal is to study the environmental performance and yield of two irrigation systems for a tomato crop fertilized with organic fertilizer from manure.

In an LCA, one of the primary purposes of a functional unit is to provide a reference to which the input and output data are normalized and allows the comparison between sys- tems [31]. Generally, LCA studies related to agricultural and horticultural systems used as functional unit those that are related with units of fruits (kg or tons) per unit of land (m² or h) [29, 30, 32-37]. Following the trend of other studies, the FU used in this study is kg of fruit m-2. All the input and outputs listed in the inventory are referred to this FU.

As defined in the ISO 14044, the system boundary (SB) is a set of criteria specifying which unit processes are part of a product system. In fact, in practical terms, SB generally is represented by a draw in which all stages and flows of the system product are shown (Fig. 2). SB also shows the inputs and outputs of the inventory (i.e. energy, water, resources

PRODUCTION OF COMPOST|CULTIVATION AND HARVESTING|CULTIVATION AND HARVESTING Raw materials|TRANSPORT OF COMPOST|Production structure Composting:||Compost -Energy -Water|"Truck Fuel Road maintenance"|Mulching -Combustible|"Truck Fuel Road maintenance"|Irrigation: -Plants (seeds) -Water -Energy Emission Compost|TRANSPORT OF SEEDS|"Plot preparation -Fuel -Tooling -Agricultural machinery" NURSERY|Truck Fuel Road maintenance|"Plot preparation -Fuel -Tooling -Agricultural machinery"
Fig. 2 System boundaries for the tomato crop

consumption, and emissions) related to the FU. Quirós et al. [22], Martínez-Blanco et al. [30], Colón et al. [38] had used a similar SB design to study the environmental assessment of horticultural systems. Furthermore, a "cradle-to-cradle" approach from the extraction of raw materials and energy production to the harvesting of the fruits was considered. Cradle- to-cradle approach is aligned with CE principles since the system considers the valoriza- tion and treatment of manure (i.e. a waste from livestock) to produce a kind of compost to be applied in a tomato crop.

Data quality and origin is one of the main requirements in LCA studies [31]. Most of the data relating to agricultural practices were obtained directly in the experimental fields (i.e. primary data) but when data was not available, some references (i.e. secondary data) were used and the Ecoinvent Database V. 3.1. Ecoinvent was used for most of the processes modeled in the product system. The data collected in the field crops are according to the best available techniques for integrated agriculture [39].

The inventories were built based on the guidelines in the ISO 14044 [40]. The Life Cycle Inventory Analysis phase (LCIA) is the second phase of LCA. LCIA refers to the inventory of the inputs/outputs data (energy, water, and raw materials). It involves the collection of the data necessary to meet the goals of the defined study [31]. As shown in Fig. 2, the system product is split into three main stages: manure compost production and transport from the production site to experimental plots, nursery (seedling) and transporta- tion to experimental plots, and the cultivation and harvesting phases. Then, the compost production stage comprises the raw material (manure), energy, and materials used in the process. The transportation of manure compost and seedlings includes the truck, fuel, and road (built and maintained). For the cultivation and harvesting stages, several elements are considered: irrigation, plot preparation, application of manure compost to plots, structure for the protection of the crop, water, fuels, agricultural machinery, and tooling. The com- post application takes into consideration the use of the machine (tractor and mixer) and the fuel consumed.

The ISO 14044 was used as the mainframe to do the environmental assessment of the crops. The ISO 14044 states four main steps to design an LCA: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact calcula- tion, and interpretation. The present experiment was led through the four steps mentioned.

The goal and scope definition are key points in an LCA. The goal is related to the intended application, the reason for carrying out the study, and the intended audience, among others [31]. The scope is related to relevant aspects of the LCA (function, func- tional unit, system boundaries, methodological aspects, etc.). In our case study, the goal is to study the environmental performance and yield of two irrigation systems for a tomato crop fertilized with organic fertilizer from manure.

In an LCA, one of the primary purposes of a functional unit is to provide a reference to which the input and output data are normalized and allows the comparison between sys- tems [31]. Generally, LCA studies related to agricultural and horticultural systems used as functional unit those that are related with units of fruits (kg or tons) per unit of land (m² or h) [29, 30, 32-37]. Following the trend of other studies, the FU used in this study is kg of fruit m-2. All the input and outputs listed in the inventory are referred to this FU.

As defined in the ISO 14044, the system boundary (SB) is a set of criteria specifying which unit processes are part of a product system. In fact, in practical terms, SB generally is represented by a draw in which all stages and flows of the system product are shown (Fig. 2). SB also shows the inputs and outputs of the inventory (i.e. energy, water, resources

consumption, and emissions) related to the FU. Quirós et al. [22], Martínez-Blanco et al. [30], Colón et al. [38] had used a similar SB design to study the environmental assessment of horticultural systems. Furthermore, a "cradle-to-cradle" approach from the extraction of raw materials and energy production to the harvesting of the fruits was considered. Cradle- to-cradle approach is aligned with CE principles since the system considers the valoriza- tion and treatment of manure (i.e. a waste from livestock) to produce a kind of compost to be applied in a tomato crop.

Data quality and origin is one of the main requirements in LCA studies [31]. Most of the data relating to agricultural practices were obtained directly in the experimental fields (i.e. primary data) but when data was not available, some references (i.e. secondary data) were used and the Ecoinvent Database V. 3.1. Ecoinvent was used for most of the processes modeled in the product system. The data collected in the field crops are according to the best available techniques for integrated agriculture [39].

The inventories were built based on the guidelines in the ISO 14044 [40]. The Life Cycle Inventory Analysis phase (LCIA) is the second phase of LCA. LCIA refers to the inventory of the inputs/outputs data (energy, water, and raw materials). It involves the collection of the data necessary to meet the goals of the defined study [31]. As shown in Fig. 2, the system product is split into three main stages: manure compost production and transport from the production site to experimental plots, nursery (seedling) and transporta- tion to experimental plots, and the cultivation and harvesting phases. Then, the compost production stage comprises the raw material (manure), energy, and materials used in the process. The transportation of manure compost and seedlings includes the truck, fuel, and road (built and maintained). For the cultivation and harvesting stages, several elements are considered: irrigation, plot preparation, application of manure compost to plots, structure for the protection of the crop, water, fuels, agricultural machinery, and tooling. The com- post application takes into consideration the use of the machine (tractor and mixer) and the fuel consumed.
13: This section contains a broad explanation of the LCA which is the methodology used to calculate the environmental impacts of the agronomical system. All the steps of the LCA are broadly explained and adapted for the experimental study. The methodology used to carry out the study is according to several LCA studies [22, 29, 30]. Also, the circular- ity indicator is calculated to know how circular is the process. To evaluate the sustain- ability of the environmental impact of GHG emissions, energy consumption for mixing and aeration, and production cost of the green compost production chains, several LCA models were applied in many papers reviewed by [13]. These models take into considera- tion the manure processing technologies along the entire life cycle of manure and related end products, industrial composting and waste management systems, biowaste treatments, and food-waste home composting systems. As shown in Fig. 1, the most famous and wide- spread circular model archetype is the Ellen MacArthur Foundation [1] diagram. This model is characterized by the division between biological and technical materials, which, with their cycles, provide the scheme with a particular shape, also called "CE butterfly dia- gram." In particular, the CE concept is based on natural principles: it takes insights from living systems, is characterized by adaptability and resilience, and follows the cascading of materials.

15: Some aspects affect the nutritional quality of crops. The nutritional quality of tomatoes may be affected by the amount of water applied, regardless of fertilizer management, and their irrigation system [40]. Dorais et al. [40] state that water deficit and poor water qual- ity are the main factors limiting worldwide crop productivity and food quality in terms of nutritional value and food safety. Precise irrigation scheduling to optimize water supply to the plant is also important for maximizing tomato nutritional value. The irrigation system includes all necessary elements used in horticultural systems [41, 42] such as pipes, pumps, and spreaders to distribute water from the source to the crops. As mentioned, two irrigation systems (i.e. integrated and dripping) were used to apply water to the tomato crop plots.
16: The manure compost was used for the fertilization of tomato crops. This compost was produced on an organic calf farm. The manure compost was produced from a composting plant located in San Marti d'Albars, province of Barcelona. A distance of 117 km from San Marti d'Albars to the plots located in Villanova l'Aguda, Lleida Province, Catalunya, was

considered. The compost is applied to the farm every two years, approximately two weeks before the transplanting of the plants. The quantity of compost applied to the crops was approximately 10 ton ha-1; this application represents about 170 kg N ha-1 year-1 which is the permitted dose of N accordingly to the European Union Directive [43]. According to the laboratory analysis [44], the manure compost presented the following characteris- tics: 42.5% of dry matter, pH of 8.6, the specific electrical conductivity of 3.9 dS/cm, the nitrogen of 3.76% ss, organic matter 87.1 ss, carbon/nitrogen ratio 11, 8. Manure compost efficiency (kg of compost ton of manure-1 was 70% [45]. Regarding emissions of the composting process, a lack of literature was observed, for the case of study. Emissions of the composting process (NH3 and N20) were calculated according to [46, 47] guidelines. Likewise, the emissions reported by [48] were used as references for the calculation of the environmental impacts: 168 kg of CO2 ton of manure-¹, 8.1 kg of CH4 ton of manure-¹ and 0.190 kg of N2O ton of manure-¹. On the other hand, there is no trustworthy for the N3H emissions to air and soil. This compost was produced following the same methodol- ogy (i.e. experimental design) used by [22, 42] whose production process was based on the composting of the organic fraction of municipal solid waste. The same assumption for energy, raw materials, water, and tootling was considered for manure compost production.

18: The experiment was developed in an organic farm located in Vilanova L'Aquila, a city located in the Noguera area, in the province of Lleida, Catalunya, Spain. The farm, where the crop was developed, has an extension of 360 m-2. The tomato plants were grown with a separation of 0.9 m and a separation of 1.2 m between rows. The density of plants was 0.92 plants m-2. To protect the plot from rain and temperature, a special structure of poly- ethylene anti-friction grid supported by wooden posts treated and supported by steel rods was designed and built. For weed protection, the plot was covered with a perforated shred- ded polyethylene sheet placed in the rows to allow transplantation at the proper distance to guarantee the optimal crop conditions such as aeration, water, and fertilizer supply. The experiment consisted of two blocks (B1 and B2) characterized by two traditional varieties of Penjar Tomato (Punxa and Plana) which are well known in Catalunya. Plana tomato variety was used as a crop reference to observe and compare with the Punxa tomato crop for the water applied, both crops were designed and grown on similar experimental condi- tions. The two different irrigation systems (integrated and dripping) have been built in each of the two blocks (B) designed for the two irrigation systems, the dripping system (D1, D2, D3, D4) and integrated system (I1, I2, I3, I4). Therefore, the experiments consisted of eight treatments (B1D1, B1D2, B1I1, B112, B2D3, B2D4, B2I3, B214). The integrated system was characterized by a distance between the drippers of 15 cm with a flow water rate of 1.21h-1; meanwhile, for the dripping system, the distance between the drippers (one drip- per per plant) was 0.9 m with a flow of h-¹.

The climate is the continental Mediterranean, with an average rainfall of 400 to 550 mm; the rainfall occurred mainly in the spring season. In the case study, the average temperature ranged from 3 to 5 °C during winter and 23 to 25 °C during the spring season [49]. The farm is located 395 m above sea level. The soil has a silky texture with a moderately basic pH (8.28), a non-limiting specific electrical conductivity (0.39 dS/m), an average content of organic matter (2.23%) [44].

20: This phase of LCA aims at understanding and evaluating the magnitude and significance of the potential environmental impacts for a product system throughout the life cycle of the product. ReCipe 2016 version 1.05 (midpoint method, hierarchies' version) meth- odology was used to calculate the environmental impacts. Two main critical catego- ries (water depletion and climate change) were selected to perform the environmental assessment of the crops for the two irrigation systems. The selected categories were climate change (CC), ozone depletion (OD), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), water depletion (WD), and fossil depletion (FD). The SimaPro Software V. 7.3.2 [50] was used for the impact analysis, with the obligatory classification and characterization phases defined by the ISO 14044 [30] Data of consumptions (energy, raw material, and water) used in the systems were collected directly in the crop fields and according to the experimental design. As is was mentioned, overall, the primary data were collected directly from the crop fields, in the case of lack of data, secondary references were used to calculate the resource consump- tions and emissions. Most of the processes were modeled using the Ecoinvent Database v3.6 [51]
21: Ellen MacArthur Foundation [1] proposed a Material Circularity Indicator (MCI). MCI indicates the "degree of circularity" of materials composing products. It has some simi- larities with life cycle assessment (LCA), but it is more focused on the flow of materi- als; therefore, it can be considered complementary to LCA. The methodology of MCI is designed for technical cycles and materials from non-renewable sources only. LCA is suggested along with a circularity assessment. The application of the LCA allows considering other impacts not included in the MCI, such as energy consumption in the process of the water used for cleaning operations, which are not taken into considera- tion. For our case of study, the circularity is the recovery (i.e. valorization and treat- ment) of livestock manure to produce a kind of organic fertilizer (i.e. compost) that was applied to a tomato crop. A simple equation was applied to measure the circular- ity of the recovered material = MCI is a parameter that repre- sents the use of waste to produce a useful product. Therefore, MCI is related to the efficiency of the composting process that measures the quantity of material recovered by the manure transformation. If MCI= 1 it is considered a total circularity that means full use of waste, on the other hand, if MCI there is no circularity that means no use of the waste, where.

Mrec = material recovered (organic fertilizer). Minput -1 = material input (livestock manure). Material recovered (Mrec)means the manure that is transformed to compost and mate- rial input (Minput) is the total manure to be composted. This MCI senses that we are tak- ing the approach of a waste that is traditionally landfilled or verted in rivers. Therefore, according to the CE principles, the production and application of the compost-manure in the soil to fertilize the tomato crop are a way to keep the livestock waste into the productive system in circular-short cycles. Likewise, the use of manure represents an

economic benefit to farmers due to the substitution of mineral fertilizers that have not only a high price but also a high energy consumption product and the consequent dam- age to the environment due to emissions pos cultivation.

23: <header>Results and Discussion </header>

This section presents the results split into two main aspects: (a) the quantity of water applied for each fertigation technique (integrated and dripping) and their effects on the yield of fruits and (b) the environmental impacts related for both systems focused mainly on the climate change and water depletion categories.

Some factors affect the nutritional quality of crops. As mentioned, the nutritional qual- ity of tomatoes may be affected by the amount of water applied, regardless of the fertili- zation management, and their irrigation system. Precise irrigation scheduling to optimize water supply to the plant is also important for maximizing tomato nutritional value. As a result of the experiment, it is remarkable the quantity of water applied to the crop and the effect on the yield. Table 1 shows the water applied for each treatment (integrated and drip- ping) and the water applied for each one.

As shown in Table 1, a comparison between the irrigation systems shows that the water applied to the integrated system was two times higher than the dripping system for both years (2014 and 2015), as well as a comparison between both years shows that in 2015, the water applied to the crops were 58% and 59% higher than 2014 for both systems (integrated and dripping), respectively. On the other hand, the rainwater (Weather Station Oliola) was similar for both years. Despite the rainwater was similar for both years (2014 and 2015), the temperature for the cropping months in 2014 was '35 °C but for 2015, the temperature for the same cropping period was a little be higher with values ranging between 35 and 39 °C, SO it is noted that in 2015, a higher water requirement was necessary during the cropping time. On the other hand, since the rainwater was almost the same for both years (Table 1), therefore, this variable did not have a significant effect on the results (i.e. yield and environmental impacts). Likewise, the quantity and quality of manure compost applied were the same for the two irrigations systems, SO as it will be seen later, the effect of a higher quantity of irrigation water had a direct effect on the yield of the fruits.

Yield is one of the most important variables in economic terms for the farmers, regions, and countries. This is an important principle of CE which considers not only the circular- ity of material into the production system but also the economic value of the activity. The yield varies according to the type of fertilizer applied, the quantity and quality of water applied, the crop management, the type of crop, the weather conditions, among others. As shown in Table 2, in the present study, the yields observed were significantly different for the two irrigation systems and both years.
Table 1 Water applied for each irrigation system for 2014 and 2015
Year|Irrigation system|Irrigation system|%|Rain water Year|Integrated|Dripping|%|Rain water 2014|167.00|78.1|114%|163.8 2015|263.70|124.1|112%|162.4 %|58%|59%||
1 Weather Station Oliola, Lleida (2015)
Table 2 Total yield for irrigation and integrated systems for years 2014-2015

Irrigation system|Total yield (kg m-2)|Total yield (kg m-2)|Total yield (kg m-2)|m Commercial -2) yield (kg|m Commercial -2) yield (kg|m Commercial -2) yield (kg Irrigation system|2014|2015|%|2014|2015|% Integrated|3.7291|7.9326|113%|3.5474|5.5325|56% Dripping|4.5532|4.7915|5%|4.4648|2.9195|- 35% %|22%|-40%||26%|-47%|

Overall, in comparison for the two years, it can be observed that the integrated sys- tem registered the best result (113% and 56%) for the total and commercial yield, respec- tively. Conversely, for the dripping system, the total yield was almost the same for 2014 and 2015 but lower for the commercial yield for the two considered years (2014 and 2015). As presented in Table 1, a higher quantity of water was applied in 2015 for both systems (integrated and dripping) but the quantity was considerably higher for the integrated sys- tem (> 58%). The case was a little different when the two irrigation systems are compared (columns), for example, in 2014, the dripping system was a higher yield than integrated for both total and commercial yield (22% and 26%) but the result changed for 2015 in which the yield was higher for an integrated system than dripping system (40% and 47%) for total and commercial yield. As explained before, the quantity of water applied in 2015 which was higher affected positively the yield of the crops. As it is shown in Table 1, the integrated system was considerably more irrigated than the dripping one. In brief, it is observed that the water applied due to the weather conditions and type of irrigations sys- tems affect the yield of the tomatoes.

A statistics analysis was performed for the two critical variables (i.e. water applied and yield) for both irrigation systems (integrated and dripping) which are closely interrelated and affect the environmental performance and yield. A robust analysis was considered to know the statistical significance of the data. Statistics is a powerful tool to understand some characteristics of the results obtained and to find deviations that can be treated and solved in future works. For our case of study, considering that there is not enough data to approximate a known distribution (i.e. standard, lognormal SO forth), a non-parametric test was performed for the experiment. Two non-parametric tests, the Kruskal Wallis [52] and Friedman Test [53] were chosen to know the significance of the median and variance of
Fig. 3 Kruskal-Wallis test: total yield versus irrigation systems
Boxplot of Total yield Integrated, Total yield Dripping
Total yield Integrated
Total yield Dripping

values for water applied and the total yield for both irrigation systems. According to [52], the median is the best parameter for measuring the central tendency of a group of data. For the case of the total yield observed, Fig. 3 shows the result of the test and the statisti- cal analysis for the median for both irrigation systems (irrigated and dripping). As it is shown, the null hypothesis confirms equivalence for the median for the yields observed in the experiment. This result means that there are no significant differences between the yields values medians, SO it can confirm that yields are equivalent (95% of significance).
Kruskal-Wallis Test: Total yield versus Irrigation system
Kruskal-Wallis Test on Total yield
Irrigation|||Ave| system|N|Median|Rank|Z Dripping|2|4.672|2.5|0.00 Integrated|2|5.831|2.5|0.00 Overall|4||2.5|
* NOTE * One or more small samples
Since P = 1, the null hypothesis is accepted. The median for the two yields obtained in the experiment are equivalent.

Additionally, the data for water applied to crops and yield were statistically assessed to know the variance of the results. Since the data are few and the distribution is unknown, the Bonferroni test [52] is a robust non-parametric test recommended for these cases. Bon- ferroni's method provides a pairwise comparison of the means. To determine which means are significantly different, we must compare all pairs. There are k = (a) (a - 1)/2 possible pairs where a = the number of treatments. In this example, a=4, SO there are 4(4 - 1)/2=6 pairwise differences to consider [52], The following equation is used to determine the pooled variance:
Fig. 4 Variance test for total yield versus irrigation system
Test for Equal Variances: Total yield vs Irrigation system
Multiple comparison intervals for the standard deviation, a = 0,05
Multiple Comparisons
P-Value 0.010
Dripping
Integrated
If intervals do not overlap, the corresponding stdevs are significantly different.

where N; is the sample size and Sj is the standard deviation for the ith sample. The standard deviations are given in the table above. For our case study, Fig. 4 and data analysis show the results for the experimental data.

As it can be seen, for a confined level of 97.5%, the null hypothesis is rejected which means that data dispersion for the two variables (i.e. total yield and two irrigation systems) are different. The randomness of the experiment demonstrates that the variances related to the yield are different for both systems (integrated and dripping). According to the lit- erature [52], Montgomery establishes that more samples are required to improve the reli- ability of the experiment in statistical terms (i.e. dispersion). Another tomato variety (i.e. Plana) showed a significant difference for Punxa variety. Both crops (Plana and Punxa) were ground under similar experimental conditions and for the same period (2014-2015). The results showed that yield for Punxa variety was eight times higher than Plana for the integrated system (4.79 kg m versus 0.66 kg m-2, and for the dripping system, Punxa was one time higher than Plana variety (7.93 kg m -2 versus 4.80 kg m-2). Considering that the water applied was the same for both varieties (Punxa and Plana) and both irrigation systems, the huge yield differences observed in the two varieties are mainly explained due to the Punxa variety is traditionally a less water consumption than Plana, and also, Punxa is a more resistant variety to the high temperatures, like the ones registered in 2015. These results are comparable with other published works developed by [22, 54, 55] in which sig- nificant yields differences were observed for Punxa (tomato) related to other tomato varie- ties grown in Catalunya under similar experimental conditions.

Additionally, the data for water applied to crops and yield were statistically assessed to know the variance of the results. Since the data are few and the distribution is unknown, the Bonferroni test [52] is a robust non-parametric test recommended for these cases. Bon- ferroni's method provides a pairwise comparison of the means. To determine which means are significantly different, we must compare all pairs. There are k = (a) (a - 1)/2 possible pairs where a = the number of treatments. In this example, a=4, SO there are 4(4 - 1)/2=6 pairwise differences to consider [52], The following equation is used to determine the pooled variance:

Test for Equal Variances: Total yield vs Irrigation system

Multiple Comparisons
P-Value 0.010
Dripping
Integrated
If intervals do not overlap, the corresponding stdevs are significantly different.

where N; is the sample size and Sj is the standard deviation for the ith sample. The standard deviations are given in the table above. For our case study, Fig. 4 and data analysis show the results for the experimental data.

As it can be seen, for a confined level of 97.5%, the null hypothesis is rejected which means that data dispersion for the two variables (i.e. total yield and two irrigation systems) are different. The randomness of the experiment demonstrates that the variances related to the yield are different for both systems (integrated and dripping). According to the lit- erature [52], Montgomery establishes that more samples are required to improve the reli- ability of the experiment in statistical terms (i.e. dispersion). Another tomato variety (i.e. Plana) showed a significant difference for Punxa variety. Both crops (Plana and Punxa) were ground under similar experimental conditions and for the same period (2014-2015). The results showed that yield for Punxa variety was eight times higher than Plana for the integrated system (4.79 kg m versus 0.66 kg m-2, and for the dripping system, Punxa was one time higher than Plana variety (7.93 kg m -2 versus 4.80 kg m-2). Considering that the water applied was the same for both varieties (Punxa and Plana) and both irrigation systems, the huge yield differences observed in the two varieties are mainly explained due to the Punxa variety is traditionally a less water consumption than Plana, and also, Punxa is a more resistant variety to the high temperatures, like the ones registered in 2015. These results are comparable with other published works developed by [22, 54, 55] in which sig- nificant yields differences were observed for Punxa (tomato) related to other tomato varie- ties grown in Catalunya under similar experimental conditions.
27: <header>Results and Discussion </header>

This section presents the results split into two main aspects: (a) the quantity of water applied for each fertigation technique (integrated and dripping) and their effects on the yield of fruits and (b) the environmental impacts related for both systems focused mainly on the climate change and water depletion categories.

Some factors affect the nutritional quality of crops. As mentioned, the nutritional qual- ity of tomatoes may be affected by the amount of water applied, regardless of the fertili- zation management, and their irrigation system. Precise irrigation scheduling to optimize water supply to the plant is also important for maximizing tomato nutritional value. As a result of the experiment, it is remarkable the quantity of water applied to the crop and the effect on the yield. Table 1 shows the water applied for each treatment (integrated and drip- ping) and the water applied for each one.

As shown in Table 1, a comparison between the irrigation systems shows that the water applied to the integrated system was two times higher than the dripping system for both years (2014 and 2015), as well as a comparison between both years shows that in 2015, the water applied to the crops were 58% and 59% higher than 2014 for both systems (integrated and dripping), respectively. On the other hand, the rainwater (Weather Station Oliola) was similar for both years. Despite the rainwater was similar for both years (2014 and 2015), the temperature for the cropping months in 2014 was '35 °C but for 2015, the temperature for the same cropping period was a little be higher with values ranging between 35 and 39 °C, SO it is noted that in 2015, a higher water requirement was necessary during the cropping time. On the other hand, since the rainwater was almost the same for both years (Table 1), therefore, this variable did not have a significant effect on the results (i.e. yield and environmental impacts). Likewise, the quantity and quality of manure compost applied were the same for the two irrigations systems, SO as it will be seen later, the effect of a higher quantity of irrigation water had a direct effect on the yield of the fruits.

Yield is one of the most important variables in economic terms for the farmers, regions, and countries. This is an important principle of CE which considers not only the circular- ity of material into the production system but also the economic value of the activity. The yield varies according to the type of fertilizer applied, the quantity and quality of water applied, the crop management, the type of crop, the weather conditions, among others. As shown in Table 2, in the present study, the yields observed were significantly different for the two irrigation systems and both years.
Table 1 Water applied for each irrigation system for 2014 and 2015
Year|Irrigation system|Irrigation system|%|Rain water Year|Integrated|Dripping|%|Rain water 2014|167.00|78.1|114%|163.8 2015|263.70|124.1|112%|162.4 %|58%|59%||
1 Weather Station Oliola, Lleida (2015)
Table 2 Total yield for irrigation and integrated systems for years 2014-2015

Irrigation system|Total yield (kg m-2)|Total yield (kg m-2)|Total yield (kg m-2)|m Commercial -2) yield (kg|m Commercial -2) yield (kg|m Commercial -2) yield (kg Irrigation system|2014|2015|%|2014|2015|% Integrated|3.7291|7.9326|113%|3.5474|5.5325|56% Dripping|4.5532|4.7915|5%|4.4648|2.9195|- 35% %|22%|-40%||26%|-47%|

Overall, in comparison for the two years, it can be observed that the integrated sys- tem registered the best result (113% and 56%) for the total and commercial yield, respec- tively. Conversely, for the dripping system, the total yield was almost the same for 2014 and 2015 but lower for the commercial yield for the two considered years (2014 and 2015). As presented in Table 1, a higher quantity of water was applied in 2015 for both systems (integrated and dripping) but the quantity was considerably higher for the integrated sys- tem (> 58%). The case was a little different when the two irrigation systems are compared (columns), for example, in 2014, the dripping system was a higher yield than integrated for both total and commercial yield (22% and 26%) but the result changed for 2015 in which the yield was higher for an integrated system than dripping system (40% and 47%) for total and commercial yield. As explained before, the quantity of water applied in 2015 which was higher affected positively the yield of the crops. As it is shown in Table 1, the integrated system was considerably more irrigated than the dripping one. In brief, it is observed that the water applied due to the weather conditions and type of irrigations sys- tems affect the yield of the tomatoes.

A statistics analysis was performed for the two critical variables (i.e. water applied and yield) for both irrigation systems (integrated and dripping) which are closely interrelated and affect the environmental performance and yield. A robust analysis was considered to know the statistical significance of the data. Statistics is a powerful tool to understand some characteristics of the results obtained and to find deviations that can be treated and solved in future works. For our case of study, considering that there is not enough data to approximate a known distribution (i.e. standard, lognormal SO forth), a non-parametric test was performed for the experiment. Two non-parametric tests, the Kruskal Wallis [52] and Friedman Test [53] were chosen to know the significance of the median and variance of

Overall, in comparison for the two years, it can be observed that the integrated sys- tem registered the best result (113% and 56%) for the total and commercial yield, respec- tively. Conversely, for the dripping system, the total yield was almost the same for 2014 and 2015 but lower for the commercial yield for the two considered years (2014 and 2015). As presented in Table 1, a higher quantity of water was applied in 2015 for both systems (integrated and dripping) but the quantity was considerably higher for the integrated sys- tem (> 58%). The case was a little different when the two irrigation systems are compared (columns), for example, in 2014, the dripping system was a higher yield than integrated for both total and commercial yield (22% and 26%) but the result changed for 2015 in which the yield was higher for an integrated system than dripping system (40% and 47%) for total and commercial yield. As explained before, the quantity of water applied in 2015 which was higher affected positively the yield of the crops. As it is shown in Table 1, the integrated system was considerably more irrigated than the dripping one. In brief, it is observed that the water applied due to the weather conditions and type of irrigations sys- tems affect the yield of the tomatoes.

A statistics analysis was performed for the two critical variables (i.e. water applied and yield) for both irrigation systems (integrated and dripping) which are closely interrelated and affect the environmental performance and yield. A robust analysis was considered to know the statistical significance of the data. Statistics is a powerful tool to understand some characteristics of the results obtained and to find deviations that can be treated and solved in future works. For our case of study, considering that there is not enough data to approximate a known distribution (i.e. standard, lognormal SO forth), a non-parametric test was performed for the experiment. Two non-parametric tests, the Kruskal Wallis [52] and Friedman Test [53] were chosen to know the significance of the median and variance of

values for water applied and the total yield for both irrigation systems. According to [52], the median is the best parameter for measuring the central tendency of a group of data. As it is shown, the null hypothesis confirms equivalence for the median for the yields observed in the experiment. This result means that there are no significant differences between the yields values medians, SO it can confirm that yields are equivalent (95% of significance).

Additionally, the data for water applied to crops and yield were statistically assessed to know the variance of the results. Since the data are few and the distribution is unknown, the Bonferroni test [52] is a robust non-parametric test recommended for these cases. Bon- ferroni's method provides a pairwise comparison of the means. To determine which means are significantly different, we must compare all pairs. There are k = (a) (a - 1)/2 possible pairs where a = the number of treatments. As it can be seen, for a confined level of 97.5%, the null hypothesis is rejected which means that data dispersion for the two variables (i.e. total yield and two irrigation systems) are different. The randomness of the experiment demonstrates that the variances related to the yield are different for both systems (integrated and dripping). According to the lit- erature [52], Montgomery establishes that more samples are required to improve the reli- ability of the experiment in statistical terms (i.e. dispersion). Another tomato variety (i.e. Plana) showed a significant difference for Punxa variety. Both crops (Plana and Punxa) were ground under similar experimental conditions and for the same period (2014-2015). The results showed that yield for Punxa variety was eight times higher than Plana for the integrated system (4.79 kg m versus 0.66 kg m-2, and for the dripping system, Punxa was one time higher than Plana variety (7.93 kg m -2 versus 4.80 kg m-2). Considering that the water applied was the same for both varieties (Punxa and Plana) and both irrigation systems, the huge yield differences observed in the two varieties are mainly explained due to the Punxa variety is traditionally a less water consumption than Plana, and also, Punxa is a more resistant variety to the high temperatures, like the ones registered in 2015. These results are comparable with other published works developed by [22, 54, 55] in which sig- nificant yields differences were observed for Punxa (tomato) related to other tomato varie- ties grown in Catalunya under similar experimental conditions.
31: <header>Results and Discussion </header>

This section presents the results split into two main aspects: (a) the quantity of water applied for each fertigation technique (integrated and dripping) and their effects on the yield of fruits and (b) the environmental impacts related for both systems focused mainly on the climate change and water depletion categories.

Some factors affect the nutritional quality of crops. As mentioned, the nutritional qual- ity of tomatoes may be affected by the amount of water applied, regardless of the fertili- zation management, and their irrigation system. Precise irrigation scheduling to optimize water supply to the plant is also important for maximizing tomato nutritional value. As a result of the experiment, it is remarkable the quantity of water applied to the crop and the effect on the yield. Table 1 shows the water applied for each treatment (integrated and drip- ping) and the water applied for each one.

As shown in Table 1, a comparison between the irrigation systems shows that the water applied to the integrated system was two times higher than the dripping system for both years (2014 and 2015), as well as a comparison between both years shows that in 2015, the water applied to the crops were 58% and 59% higher than 2014 for both systems (integrated and dripping), respectively. On the other hand, the rainwater (Weather Station Oliola) was similar for both years. Despite the rainwater was similar for both years (2014 and 2015), the temperature for the cropping months in 2014 was '35 °C but for 2015, the temperature for the same cropping period was a little be higher with values ranging between 35 and 39 °C, SO it is noted that in 2015, a higher water requirement was necessary during the cropping time. On the other hand, since the rainwater was almost the same for both years (Table 1), therefore, this variable did not have a significant effect on the results (i.e. yield and environmental impacts). Likewise, the quantity and quality of manure compost applied were the same for the two irrigations systems, SO as it will be seen later, the effect of a higher quantity of irrigation water had a direct effect on the yield of the fruits.

Yield is one of the most important variables in economic terms for the farmers, regions, and countries. This is an important principle of CE which considers not only the circular- ity of material into the production system but also the economic value of the activity. The yield varies according to the type of fertilizer applied, the quantity and quality of water applied, the crop management, the type of crop, the weather conditions, among others. As shown in Table 2, in the present study, the yields observed were significantly different for the two irrigation systems and both years.

33: Life cycle interpretation is the final phase of the LCA procedure, in which the results of a Life Cycle Inventory Analysis are summarized and discussed as a basis for conclusions, recommendations, and decision-making by the goal and scope definition [30]. This inter- pretation plays an important role because it is the way to explain and correlate the environ- mental impacts of a product system in regard to the use of energy, resources, and water. Likewise, this explanation of impacts is extremely important to understand the stages of the system product that mostly contributes to the negative impacts. For our case study, the outcomes of the environmental impact are very important to propose solutions to adapt and

mitigate the systems to look for more sustainable agriculture. Likewise, it is very important to highlight the close relationship between the LCA and the CE thinking; the LCA deter- mines the impacts and measures to mitigate the impacts through its life cycle, SO it could be reached if the waste (i.e. manure) is reinserted to the economic system in a close or open- loop strategy.

From Table 3 and Fig. 5, it is possible to compare the environmental impacts for the two irrigations systems. It is observed that the dripping systems have a greater contribution (40% higher) for all impact categories studies except for the water depletion. The differ- ences are mainly explained by the yield which is related to the functional unit (kg m-2) obtained in the two irrigation systems. As shown in Table 3, for the year 2015, the inte- grated system had a yield of 7.932 kg of fruits m-2; meanwhile, the dripping system had a yield of 4.732 kg of fruits m-2; therefore, as explained anywhere, in LCI (i.e. energy, materials, and water consumption), it can be concluded a lower impact contribution per category and FU for the highest yield. Conversely, the integrated system had a higher water consumption which shows a greater water depletion than the dripping system (Fig. 5).

Climate change and water scarcity are the two critical impacts of ecosystem life and the main concern for society in terms of sustainability. For this reason, those two cate- gories were chosen to understand impact contribution per stage considered in the LCIA
Fig. 7 Water depletion category for a tomato crop fertilized with manure compost

(Fig. 6). The environmental impacts analysis per stage is a deep approach to understand which of them are the highest contributors for a specific category [30]. As shown in the last section, this assessment permits to focus specifically on those highest contribu- tors in the product-system to look for good agricultural practices, for example, efficient use of the energy to reduce the CO2 emissions, and optimization of the irrigation water applied to crops, the waste management for recycling. The climate change category (CC) is related to emissions of greenhouse gases to the air; this category is expressed in CO2 eq.; climate change can result in adverse effects upon ecosystem health, human health, and material welfare. Figure 6 shows the main contributors per m² 2 for the CC; the structure to protect the crop (36%), mulching (25%), and compost production (29%) are the higher contributors for the CC category. Within the protection structure and mulching stage, the highest contributors were the steel used to support the structure and the high-density polyethylene (HDPE) to protect the crop. In regard to the composting process, the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 are the main contributors to this process. Overall, in the environmental impacts per stage, a similar trend (i.e. impact contribu- tors) was observed for all categories considered in this study, although the contribution depended on the impact category considered.

The water depletion category is related to the vulnerability of basins to freshwater depletion. Based on local blue water scarcity, the water depletion index (WDI) denotes the risk that water consumption can lead to the depletion of freshwater resources. The water depletion is measured in m3 of the water applied to the crop. As shown in Fig. 7, the main contributor to the water depletion category was the irrigation water applied to crops.

As shown in the last section, this assessment permits to focus specifically on those highest contribu- tors in the product-system to look for good agricultural practices, for example, efficient use of the energy to reduce the CO2 emissions, and optimization of the irrigation water applied to crops, the waste management for recycling. The climate change category (CC) is related to emissions of greenhouse gases to the air; this category is expressed in CO2 eq.; climate change can result in adverse effects upon ecosystem health, human health, and material welfare. Figure 6 shows the main contributors per m² 2 for the CC; the structure to protect the crop (36%), mulching (25%), and compost production (29%) are the higher contributors for the CC category. Within the protection structure and mulching stage, the highest contributors were the steel used to support the structure and the high-density polyethylene (HDPE) to protect the crop. In regard to the composting process, the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 are the main contributors to this process. Overall, in the environmental impacts per stage, a similar trend (i.e. impact contribu- tors) was observed for all categories considered in this study, although the contribution depended on the impact category considered.

The water depletion category is related to the vulnerability of basins to freshwater depletion. Based on local blue water scarcity, the water depletion index (WDI) denotes the risk that water consumption can lead to the depletion of freshwater resources. The water depletion is measured in m3 of the water applied to the crop. As shown in Fig. 7, the main contributor to the water depletion category was the irrigation water applied to crops.
2: Fig. 6 Impact per stage for climate change

mitigate the systems to look for more sustainable agriculture. Likewise, it is very important to highlight the close relationship between the LCA and the CE thinking; the LCA deter- mines the impacts and measures to mitigate the impacts through its life cycle, SO it could be reached if the waste (i.e. manure) is reinserted to the economic system in a close or open- loop strategy.

From Table 3 and Fig. 5, it is possible to compare the environmental impacts for the two irrigations systems. It is observed that the dripping systems have a greater contribution (40% higher) for all impact categories studies except for the water depletion. The differ- ences are mainly explained by the yield which is related to the functional unit (kg m-2) obtained in the two irrigation systems. As shown in Table 3, for the year 2015, the inte- grated system had a yield of 7.932 kg of fruits m-2; meanwhile, the dripping system had a yield of 4.732 kg of fruits m-2; therefore, as explained anywhere, in LCI (i.e. energy, materials, and water consumption), it can be concluded a lower impact contribution per category and FU for the highest yield. Conversely, the integrated system had a higher water consumption which shows a greater water depletion than the dripping system (Fig. 5).

5: As explained before a circularity indicator was calculated according to CE principles (MCI = M rec Min ). MCI for the manure compost was 65% > to < 70% which means that our process has a high circularity for the sample studied. This MCI is assimilated to the efficiency of the process. Therefore, considering that all the compost manure is applied
to crops which means a high index of circularity. Eghball et al. [45] found an efficiency of 70% (kg compost ton of manure-1 which is the range of the value found in the current study.
6: The increasing need for CE drives on-farm composting of agricultural biomass waste as a viable option to enhance soil quality, plant health, and crop protection due to its multi- ple beneficial effects. Crops yield is dependent on several variables such as water applied, fertilizers, crop management, weather conditions, and type of crop. In the case study, the water applied varied for the two years and the irrigation systems considered (i.e. integrated and dripping). Therefore, under the same experimental conditions for the two irrigation systems, the water applied (1 m -2) for 2015 was 58% and 59% higher than 2014 for the integrated and dripping system, respectively. The weather conditions for both years and the type of irrigation were the variables that mainly influenced the water supply to crops. Considering that the water supplied to crop is one of the main variables that influence the yield, SO for the year 2015, the yield observed for 2015 was 113% and 5% than 2014 for the integrated and dripping system, respectively. In regard to the organic fertilizer applied to crops, a study realized through the scientific literature shows the fertilizer organic produced from manure yielded better than other organic fertilizers (ex. compost from municipal solid waste). Overall, the analysis of the environmental impacts for 2015 showed that the inte- grated system had a better environmental performance than the dripping system for the six impact categories selected for the study except for the water depletion which was 37% higher for the irrigation system. Likewise, the environmental impacts per stage for the two main critical impact categories selected (i.e. climate change and water depletion) showed that the structure used to protect the crop (36%), mulching (25%), and compost production (19%) were the main contributors for the climate change category, and for the water, deple- tion is understandable that the water applied to crop (i.e. irrigation stage) represented the main contributor (> 95%). Most of the organic fertilizer is reintroduced into the productive system in a circular loop. The circularity indicator of 70% is high (> 50%) considering that most of the manure is transformed into organic fertilizer due to the microbial action. Fur- ther studies and research are recommended to measure and compare different crop systems and to assess the environmental impact.
